reviews.review: Same issues as the last game. But now you have a novelty building mechanic too. Stuff disappears as soon as you look away which makes building anything half decent a chore. Very disappointing game.

Another **** masterpiece, Besides the PTSD i got from (yet another electric boss) Queen Gibdo. But besides that, its a masterpiece and NOT a DLC.
ChoppedFish out!
The gameplay aspect is incredible but the story, characters and dungeons are really bland in my opinion

I committed the sin of playing this game before BotW because I didn't play many LoZ games before anyway, but this is one of the best open world games I've played, period.
This game has a decent story, nice visuals/environment, and a great soundtrack.
Though the game is a bit buggy sometimes, I can't think of anything worth considering docking a point. I still feel this way after completing most of the game (beat all shrines, all bosses/minibosses with medals etc) and I look forward to replaying the game in a couple years.
Now that I have played BotW I do still prefer the zonai devices/abilities here, but I think they are both about the same level of epicness, just slightly different atmospheres.

The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom, on the other hand, represents the opposite extreme of Ocarina of Time: instead of perfecting the classic structure, it expands it into an open system where physics, creativity, and experimentation are the true language of design. What's astonishing is that, despite the enormous risk of disrupting its own equilibrium—more freedom, more systems, more potential chaos—the game doesn't fall apart. Instead, it maintains cohesion, purpose, and consistent reward, demonstrating that the series can risk everything and still sustain itself with impressive clarity of vision. The Zelda series could falter when it becomes overly conscious of its own structure—as happens in The Legend of Zelda: Skyward Sword, where progression feels excessively programmatic and directed—but what's extraordinary is that when it finds the balance between design and intention, between freedom and form, between homage and identity, it doesn't fail. Whether from the classic precision of Twilight Princess or the systemic audacity of Tears of the Kingdom, Zelda demonstrates that it can wander down radically different paths without losing its essence.

I understand everything that this game gets wrong. There are many problems with the world design, the story, the characters, the shrines, the dungeons, your abilities, the quests, the general game design, the cost, and so much more. I wanted to give this game a 9/10, but I couldn't. When I first played this game I fell in love with it. I may have even considered it better than Breath of the Wild in some ways. I know some people hate this game because they love Zelda, and they love BotW and feel like this game doesn't live up to all the support given by it's predecessors. I also love the Zelda series, It's my favorite video game series of all time, but I also love this game. My brain says "this game ****" and it kind of does, it really does, but I love it. So because this website counts a 7/10 as "mixed or average" I think I'll go with that.
Fabulous game. An improvement to Breath of the Wild: entertaining, creative, unique. But, as its predecessor, the world can feel empty, the story is weak, and WE WANT CLASSIC DUNGEONS TO COME BACK.
greatest game of all times. deserved goty 2023 instead of baldurs gate 3 :((

If it's your first time playing a Zelda game, then this will dive you into hours of exploration and depth. I love Tears of the Kingdom, with it's awesome characters, soundtrack, and, most of all, the story. I very much do recommend this game! As well as Breath of the Wild

ABSOLUTE VIDEOGAME 900p 30FPS!

Trust me, just play it, it's a beautiful experience!

I played it after finishing BOTW. It's my second Zelda game ever. Better than its predecessor, but not a great game. The skills and puzzles are even better than in the previous game, even with better graphics (I played on NS2) and a better story. But it's still not amazing. It's still repetitive, and you still have to read the dialogue because there's no sound. The game is better than BOTW, but still an average product.
This Game is super conperaple to breath of the wild but it sadly does NOT come close

TOTK is not as good as BOTW, but it comes close. The graphics are amazing. My favorite aspects of the game were the hand mechanics and gliding all around Hyrule. The Ultrahand mechanic in particular, was a lot of fun and added creativity to exploration. However, I found myself relying almost entirely on Ultrahand and rarely using abilities like Recall. The game should have encouraged more balanced use of its abilities.

The game offers an enormous amount of content, but much of it feels unnecessary or uninteresting. Some areas of the Depths felt empty and boring to explore, and the same was true for the sky islands. Because the surface world is very similar to BOTW, exploration didn’t feel very surprising this time around. Completing the game also felt very cumbersome and more difficult than BOTW.

In terms of story, the narrative felt stronger than in Breath of the Wild. I enjoyed collecting the Tears much more than the memories in BOTW. Regarding enemies and combat, I personally preferred the Guardians from BOTW over the Constructs. The Fuse system was a fun addition that added diversity and creativity to gameplay. Finally, the music was not memorable, and I missed the more recognizable, classic Zelda musical style.

I absolutely loved the game. Still do. It offers more verticality to explore huge Hyrule compared to BOTW: you can fly with limits and you can explore caves. It offers another set of shrines and new crafting system compared to BOTW along with vehicles invention possibilities. You can still cook the same way. It is more like an alternative game to BOTW than a sequel. For newcomers it offers a great journey. I have played it together with my 8 year old daughter and that was an experience for us both. The game requires 250 hours to explore most of the surface and sky islands and that is worth it. The only downside of the new Zelda editions are depths which we didnt't like. You can explore it just a little during the game. Still I  have to say it is the same great game as Breath of The Wild - sometimes it is even better - and sometimes it is a little worse but it is a great adventure nonetheless. Caves are awesome that is probably my favourite addition to Hyrule, quests are plenty and interesting. New abilities are strange but qool. Sky islands are great I don't mind that they are not huge. We liked the story  One of my favorite games. If you are going to play it just make sure you know how much is 250 hours. Have a great time.
The amount of freedom you have in this game is astounding, there are a million ways to solve every puzzle, go ahead and choose whichever one makes the game fun for you,

When i played Breath of the Wild, i loved it. But then Tears of the Kingdom came along and it literally blew my mind. Definitely one of my favourite games. I enjoyed playing it so much and would definitely recommend it to anyone.
Met and in often cases exceeded my expectations for this BOTW follow up. Took my back to the Hyrule I enjoyed so much in the first game, with a new adventure, side characters, and adventure.
I didn't think you can beat BOTW but this game did. It took what its predecessor did and just hit it out of the park! Masterpiece again.

Gave it a 5.6 which I'm graciously rounding up. Can be genuinely fun at times but for the most part it took BotW's world and made it very boring. Has terrible dungeons and shrines, and no visual improvements to BotW despite releasing SIX YEARS LATER
A Step up from Botw with better graphics and a new world while Botw has some other features that make it better than this, it's still a very amazing game

While totk is undoubtedly a good game, and i think the gameplay is better than botw. It takes too much from the original game that in turn makes it feel like a DLC. I wish there was more to this game and some zelda fans make too many excuses.

This game is simply perfect. The sequel to one of the best games in history (if not the best). The music, the story and the things you can do are endless in this game. I have played ~400 hours, and am still not bored. The only criticism might be its performance, since this game runs on a Nintendo Switch, but now with the Nintendo Switch 2 edition, that is 100% fixed.

Tears Of The Kingdom is more of the same and despite that it isnt an issue for me. Tears Of The Kingdom builds on Breath Of The Wild to add new fun gameplay changes. Being able to construct cool vehicles and battle machines was pretty enjoyable. I spent quite a bit of time with this. I made a roomba with lasers that could destroy an enemy camp with ease and a flying machine that gets me around.Dungeons still **** in this game and the depths and sky didnt add as much as I was hoping for but I still enjoyed this game this isnt a 10/10 like breath of the wild but they added enough new stuff for me to make it new enough for me.
Amazing sequel to a nearly perfect previous game its amazing how the devs managed to bring that same magic back for a game you can spend 100s of hours playing

Breath of the Wild was already an incredible game with so much to do and explore, but I think Tears of the Kingdom improves on it. While people complain that they feel this game just kinda ignores what happened in BOTW, that didn’t really affect my enjoyment of the game since I think the gameplay is better since you can build to make navigation much faster and you’re also introduced to the Sky Islands, (which I will admit are a little underwhelming since most of them are really tiny) and the Depths which I thought was fun to explore and opened for more exploration and I enjoyed getting all the lightroots to get more of the map. The story is this game is I think great and I especially love what they did with Zelda especially when she sacrificed herself to become the Light Dragoon and restore the Master Sword for Link. Now, I will say that the Master Sword is a downgrade in this game because you can’t upgrade the damage and durability like you could in BOTW and the story makes it seem like the Master Sword will be more powerful than ever when it pretty much has the exact same base damage of 30 and runs out of energy kinda quickly. It’s even worse when you have to either fuse things to make the sword do more damage or there’s straight up just better weapons than it. It’s just really disappointing that they nerfed the Master Sword in this game. Anyway, I wanted to talk about the dungeons in this game which I think are much better than the Divine Beasts from BOTW since they actually feel different from each other in terms of design and puzzle solving. The bosses are also more interesting this time around with my favorite being Colgera from the Wind Temple. Final thing I wanna talk is Ganondorf since they absolutely did a fantastic job with him. He just looks so badass and the final fight is so much better than Calamity Ganon from BOTW. I especially love the last phase where he turns into a literal dragon and you ride on top of the Light Dragon to hit his weak spots and it’s just so epic. Oh, I forgot to mention this, but while BOTW had the Champions, this game has Sages and while they’re cool to have, I just don’t like how you have to use most of them by walking up to them and it could get annoying especially when you have multiple out. And there’s also the fact that some of them like Sidon and Mineru I just found to be kinda useless and Riju’s power is pretty much the Shock Arrows from BOTW. My favorite to use was easily Tulin especially when used in the sky since you can get more distance with him which is funny that the wind sage is the most useful just like Revali’s Gale from BOTW. Anyway, I’m gonna wrap this up by saying that TOTK is an improvement over BOTW and I would highly recommend playing this game.

The Legend of Zelda, Tears of the Kingdom is more than just a sequel, it’s a bold step forward for a game that was already revolutionary. Building on everything Breath of the Wild achieved, it takes familiar ideas and pushes them to incredible new heights, showing that even in a world we thought we knew, there’s still so much to discover.What makes Tears of the Kingdom truly special is how deep and creative it feels. Mechanics like Ultrahand, Fuse, and Ascend turn you from an explorer into a true inventor. Every challenge can be solved in countless ways, limited only by your imagination. The physics system, already amazing in the first game, has been expanded so that experimenting with the world is endlessly rewarding. Whether you’re building complex machines, improvising weapons, or stumbling on surprising interactions, the game constantly encourages you to play in your own way.Technically, the game is impressive, but it’s important to note that on the original Switch, the game sometimes struggles due to hardware limitations. Frame drops, long load times, and occasional stuttering can make parts of the experience frustrating, and the sheer scale of the world doesn’t always feel smooth. However, playing it on the  Switch 2 shows the game at its best: stable, gorgeous, and fully immersive, making it much easier to appreciate the incredible world and systems Nintendo has **** art and sound remain incredible. Hyrule looks and feels timeless, the music and effects add subtle but powerful emotion, and the story is stronger and more refined without ever taking away your freedom to explore. Every moment feels intentional, every adventure personal.Tears of the Kingdom doesn’t just improve on its predecessor, it completely redefines what freedom and creativity in a game can feel like. It’s a landmark achievement, a masterclass in game design, and a reminder of why Nintendo does what it does best. One of the goats

The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom is a phenomenal sequel which fixed majority of the flaws that Breath of the Wild had. I much preferred going through the different Temples compared to the Divine Beasts as they all had their own unique atmosphere and vibe that wasn't able to be created within the Divine Beasts. The side quests within TOTK were also improved upon, by including Addison and helping him put up signs to helping Koroks find their friends (even if it included strapping them to a rocket). Fundamentally the game was better as well, with the abilities significantly improving on those in BOTW. Ultrahand, Recall and Ascend allowed for unlimited freedom on how to solve all the puzzles. Additionally I found TOTK's story to be more intriguing too, as it persuaded me to actually find all the geoglyphs and unlock all the memories. The ability to create your own vehicles also allowed for varied and more effective transportation, however unlike in BOTW I finally figured out how to use the horse properly. Overall, I think The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom is a MUST play game, and the definitive way to play a 'Breath of the Wild' style game on the Nintendo Switch.
The story is superbit hits in many emotional level. I know to the masses Nintendo games aren’t known for their stories but this game is worth checking out.

If you want a great adventure, Tears of the Kingdom is your game. This game is absolutely a whole experience, i had the chance to play Breath of the Wild when it came out a few years ago, and it was a masterpiece. I was afraid Tears of the Kingdom didn´t overcome the standard that BOTW created in it´s time, but it truly made it feel like a demo. The amount of creativity, passion and love in this game is seen in every minute detail, I´m still amazed that the development team made this whole game run on a Nintendo Switch. The narrative is not it´s strongest point though, they could´ve done it better in my opinion, but that´s the only "issue" the game might have besides a couple performance problems the game has due to the hardware (this olnly happening in the Nintendo Switch version). Those two little problems aren´t enough to overshadow the greatness of Tears of the Kingdom.
Finally, I was able to do the 100% of the game, and I can truly tell that it gave me 800 hours of pure fun, adventure and exploration.
If you have a Nintendo Switch, and the opportunity to play The Legend of Zelda, give Tears of the Kingdom the chance, you are not going to regret.

Tears of the Kingdom is a perfect sequel of Breath of the Wild. The game expands Hyrule more with flying sky islands underground caves and the Depths a deep underground biome filled with darkness. The game has wonderful combat, clothing, quests, and a bunch of more content around Hyrule. The story is a wonderful experience as Ganondorf is resurrected, and Link must defeat Ganondorf to save Princess Zelda as she's gone right back in time. Link and Zelda have grown closer as it seems they are taking their relations to the next and beyond. The mechanics like the fusion mechanic surely impressed me very including the successor of Magnesis, that being Ultrahand, which really helps the puzzle get done in time. Anyway, this was my full review of The Legend of Zelda Tears of the Kingdom, and if you read this review thank you

Nintendo correctly decided to evolve the physics-based gameplay of Breath of the Wild, and did so originally as an expansion pack, in lieu of a full sequel.

They then completely **** this idea up by instead making it a full sequel that entirely focused on this one mechanic that turned out to be rubbish anyway, whilst simulatenously giving us a sequel to one of the best games ever made by absolutely pissing all over it- even as far as ignoring that the first game ever happened (a sin already committed with Wind Waker's unskippable 5 minute opening cutscene 20 years prior that pissed on Ocarina of Time and the player's accomplishments).
What is new and present in TOTK is inherently interesting and dare I say fun, but the execution is so massively flawed that whatever magic the game holds is utterly marred by the focus on quantity-over-quality. I loved the first game to absolute pieces, but can't bring myself to ever play TOTK ever again. It's absolutely insane that this game took as much time as the original game despite recycling 70% of it. Easier said than done, but I think they should have focused on it as a sequel first and foremost, with the contraptions mechanic being added to in post-release updates. Downsizing the world and splitting the content equally between Sky, Land, and Underground would have been this game's biggest boon by far, and would have needed as much effort as copy-pasting literally every layer of it, which is pathetically what they did. The 100+ copy-pasted caves were also an abject mistake that I still cannot quite believe they did. For the amount of content and quality it does deliver, it is an astounding game almost beyond measure, but it's neither a game that any previous BOTW player has any business near, nor anyone looking to fully complete beyond the half-arsed story. The contraptions also effectively feel like their own game that were still tacked-on after the fact, and half of it flat-out doesn't work.
A huge step forward from BotW. This game is a pure classic and everyone enjoying open world games should play this.

TotK is easily one of the worst gaming experiences I've ever had. Everything is bureaucratic, everything is fragile, nothing offers rewards, unnecessarily crowded with enemies, everything is poorly planned and completely inexperienced. It doesn't even seem like a game built on top of BotW, which was one of the best games I've ever played.

A good follow-up, but it can’t quite recreate the impact of Breath of the Wild, which set the bar high. The expanded three-layer map — underground, surface, and sky — adds a clever twist to a world many players already knew well. Immersion remains top-tier, with incredible sound design and environmental detail. Its weakest point is the construction system: creative at first, but ultimately over-used and sometimes disruptive to the game’s flow. Even so, TOTK delivers a  memorable adventure for most players.
Botw but better in every way. Music, adventure, size, dungeons,quests,  abilities, crafting, enemies, story, characters you name it. Botw rocked but this is the definitive version
sad compared to botw I cant have fun and I think the sheer amount of zonai and how easy it is to travel takes the adventure out of hyrule

The legend of zelda tears of the kingdom is the 2023 game of the year here at the golden gamepad and its also one of the greatest games of all time. It took the world of breath of the wild that already changed the open world formula and put it to shame! The tears of the kingdom is beyond just a masterpeice, its something i can only describe as magic. Its story is heart breaking and there are so many things to do in this game that it honestly feels more real then real life! This game is a must play!
It's great but it has his flaws the sage abilities are just useless except for tulin And I think the the armor upgrades and the rupee system could have been done better
I just felt like I was playing breath of the wild again, which I already played twice.

One of my favourite games of all time, it's a nice sequel to botw and a lot of fun to explore and play with all the mechanics, I would say the only problem here is the story, it's not much and the fact that you can find the cutscenes in any order is just wrong
fun game and continuation to botw. plenty of content and the same amazing combat style. it is a fantastic switch game and everyone should play it

I was ready to experience a somehow even better game than the first based off of the reviews, and in very few ways did it actually improve upon the first. In almost every other way, it was a let down. Not a bad game by any means, I still enjoyed it

After so many years in development, the sandbox and creative system that was added.. was not enough to improve on the many issues I had with its prequel. Pretty much everything was perceived as perfect, and nothing was truly improved upon.. what a shame.

From more repetitive and dull tasks.. to the same world being re-used. It doesn't feel like this rethread of a successful product achieved anything worth praising.

When I realized I could create that "one bike" that made traversal obsolete (and horses for the most part), or launch miles into the sky to comb every pixel of the map.. my engagement disappeared. The old saying “players will optimize the fun out ****” remains true, because when something is the best option, why would you want to handicap yourself and waste time doing anything else? Especially when there's so little this glorified-DLC offered.

Everything I disliked about BotW is heightened here, which made the experience even worse as I held all those frustrations from start to finish. Good music, though!
if you compare it with botw, its like the same but with better story, more boss,dungeon,more open world, and better final boss i think
Truly a flawless game and an interesting story telling method, though not for everyone, I enjoyed the way it was told.

The follow up to my all time favorite Legend of Zelda title. Still a very good game adding a lot more to explore. The new mechanics are very nice and add another layer of possibilities in the shrines and puzzles. The sandbox aspect is done very nicely, but I prefer the normal mode of travel, by foot.
The story and especially its ending is great and the final boss battle and battles in general are more difficult than in its predecessor. Loved this game as well, but Breath of the Wild still is my favorite of all time.

Nintendo made the impossible and improved BOTW. They fixed some issues like shortcuts for items, more tutorials for new players and better stable experience. But the best part is: even bigger open world with 3 levels to explore with new mechanics and items!
its objectively a worse game than breath of the wild, but i find it more fun to play. mainly because of Fuse and the fact that bullet time only takes stamina when you shoot.
This game and its predecessor have refined the open world genre forever. Truly a masterpiece, and I hope we can see a game to round out the trilogy soon.
I did Never Play a Game Like this you can explore the world with No Limits an absolutely mind blowing game

Just like the first game already a great game. I loved the new abilities. Personally I was most excited about the sky islands, but I got a bit disappointed, since they were nearly empty and very repetitive. Sometimes the open world can feel a bit repetitive in general, especially if you played the first part, but I would still recommend it. The story is intriguing and i bit my lips while playing the highlights of the story.

Honestly I'm not sure why the internet has decided that this game **** over the last year or so. To me, even with it's shortcomings, this is a sequel SO GOOD, that I never have the desire to play Breath of The Wild again. The music is better, the dungeons are better (although still not what I'd like them to be), the abilities are more fun & lead to making more creative solutions to solve problems. I mean C'MON Y'ALL. The final boss fight alone is such a massive step up from the previous game, y'all are actually tripping balls.

(Gameplay - 9/10):
This game's gameplay is truly unique, and is doing something no other game has done before. The combat is hugely customizable, allowing you to create literally anything. The closest thing that I can relate to this system is Minecraft's redstone. If you like creative gameplay, than this is a must play.

(Story - 3/10):
This is not a story game. But most people that play Zelda games already know this - this game's story is, however, worse than the other Zelda games. If you want to play a Zelda game with a good story, play Majora's Mask.

(Overall): If you have not played Breath of the Wild yet, don't play this game. It will be very overwhelming. This might not seem like a big deal, but the appeal of Breath of the Wild was its simplicity yet huge scale--this game does not have the same satisfying simplicity. This game also takes risks in its new mechanics, which will likely impact the entire industry, but overall, it is still unpolished.
Honestly not my type of game but still a good game even if it's not my thing

Before anyone disagrees: this IS a great game, it's objectively better than breath of the wild, but that's not where my frustration lays with this game. 

My frustration comes from the fact this game feels exactly like a dlc for breath of the wild, i finished botw and have too much playtime on it (260h) so this was just more of the same thing for me. I could excuse them a little of the story was any different, or any kind of canonical connection between both games. The idea that i'd have to pay 20bucks for botw and totk if i wanted it on switch 2 in 4k is beyond me.

All of that said, if you're getting into zelda on switch, i'd get totk from the beginning. It's basically botw with more content.
90% of the game elements are recycled, repackaged, redesigned elements from botw. This game is nothing without botw.
Bought it, played 2 hours and gave up. Not for me. Crafting was painfully immersion breaking.

I like all Zelda games, and this one is no exception. But this one it's a bit difficult to review. Especially when you compare it to the predecessor game. The story is better and the gameplay is fun, but overall it feels almost the same. It's ok to reuse the same engine and world, but much of it is generic and dull. For example, the shrines are essentially identical, just with different assets. And what really annoyed me from the beginning were the constant modal popups when you pick something up, no matter how trivial it was. A simple notification would have been enough, with the option to view more details if needed. It just ends to button mashing to skip everything. Same with the shrines. A clear sign of poor UI/UX design. Some quests are also unnecessarily tedious. Instead of highlighting the location mentioned in the text, the game forces me to carefully read it. I also hope for complete voice acting in the next game. It would enhanced the experience and will work with this dialogue system, which other games have proven it. The worst part of the game are the breakable weapons and other items. And this even faster than in the previous game. And the Master Sword can't even be used to defeat a small mob because it breaks first. I understand the design, but it's just not fun. The Blood Moon event is also frustrating. It interrupts the gameplay and ruin your progress. A better design would have been respawning enemies outside your range without black screen fade. The game feels repetitive with lots of copy-paste content. Most treasure chests contain boring, useless stuff, even the hard to find ones feel unrewarding. The game forces you to heavy grind. Companions are an interesting idea, but in practice they're either in the way or too far to be helpful. I'd rather just have their abilities myself. Also, having to watch the same cutscenes from companions 4-5 times is a drag. There's a feature that highlights cave locations, which is great. But it still shows completed caves, which makes no sense. A lot of stuff just pads the playtime. And while I love spending time in this world, these kinds of issues stack up. At the end of the day, I do enjoy the game. But it carries over too many of the same flaws from its predecessor. I really miss traditional dungeons with meaningful item based progression. I'm confident the strengths of classic Zelda and modern design can be combined. I hope so for the next game. I wish I could give it a higher rating. Maybe a 8 or 9. But despite all the things it does right, the issues just weigh it down.
Shouldn’t have to buy a whole new console for it to actually be at its best. Amazing game but having to pay to play it at the framerate other games start out with is atrocious.

Zelda really ought to move faster, especially on his snowboard. It's too slow and boring. Nintendo is wasting my time by making me wait for their slow animations to complete. Do they think I have all day? I don't, and I'm not about to waste what little free time I have on watching this slow character plod about in a way that only serves to artificially extend the games length. It's also really hard to be immersed in the Zelda universe when he can climb up completely flat surfaces by grabbing non-existent hand holds. It's gotten so bad I actually have to pretend to grab the rougher bricks and act as if I will fall if I grab the flat surfaces. I shouldn't have to go to this much effort to make the game work in a realistic way. Overall, great game ruined by some easily fixable problems. I hope you're listening, Nintendo.

Tears of the Kingdom does a lot right, but unfortunately a lot of that "done right" was taken from breath of the wild. When that is taken out what you have is the underground level, which is quite obnoxious to navigate through and fells pretty unrewarding, and the sky, which is done alright, but sometimes getting from sky island A to sky island B can be tedious and take more time then its worth.

The game is an unfinished mess. While it does have some genuine fun and new ideas they are overshadowed by the large amounts of frustration, grinding, repeat ad naseum. As far as I am concerned the game is a total and complete waste of money.

"No fun allowed: The video game."

It's a step down from BOTW in every way. Everything about this game is tedious, gimmicky and unsatisfying. The sky adds nothing but more empty space, the underground is a big open bowl that constantly blocks your progress with giant walls, the story and world map are both rehashes of their predecessors, and the entire thing is a derivative asset flip with a first party price tag.

The garry's mod vehicles are gimmicks to the Nth degree, they're slow, awful to control and mandatory for most of the game. Want to build a plane to fly accross the map? Too bad, because the wing part will just vanish for no reason because no fun allowed.

Every single weapon you pick up has to have another weapon or a monster part glued onto it for it to do any damage at all, but of course weapons still break every 5 hits like in BOTW, meaning that you have to stop, drop and glue every 5 seconds to be able to contend with the many many damage sponge enemies.

The story is incredibly dumb even by nintendo standards, the flashbacks are even more annoying to find than the memories, and the sidequests feel like obligations rather than something the devs actually wanted to add. Nothing says "rewarding" like agonizingly making your way through a boring quest for 30 minutes to an hour, only to be rewarded with nothing but 100 rupees. You can get the same amount of money from breaking a rock.

It's nintendo first party sludge for diehard fanboys, plain and simple. Almost $100 for the same game, but worse. Oh, and it still runs like trash as well as the cherry on top.

This game is fine in a isolated view, but even then has it's flaws.
What bothers me the most is the utter failure it is at being a sequel to Botw. SkittyBitty's review on youtube perfectly sums up my feelings about this game.

I would not recommend this game to anyone. Not even to other big Zelda fans – Just watch a youtube video of the story and you’ll be iight. This game could’ve and should’ve fixed all the issues with BotW, but it did none of that. Instead, it copy-pasted nearly everything and padded it with a few tweaks and shoddy writing. If it wasn’t for the animators making some epics cutscenes, I may not have even beaten the damn thing.

Since the predecessor couldn’t satisfy me, I’m very disappointed that TotK is basically the same game, in the same world, doing almost the same things. After so many years of development I can’t understand why they ship the same thing in a different package. I have played so many games, this one couldn’t shine in anything and I can’t understand the rating, which never reflects the real quality of this game, but instead the hype and probably a lot of people playing an open world game for the first time. A mediocre game isn’t better just because it’s Zelda, it is still mediocre. That’s the truth. And I can’t give a second iteration of the same mediocre game the same rating as its predecessor.

This game is very strange. Whilst technically better than Breath of the Wild and possessing more content, it did not give me as much enjoyment. The first 30 - 40 hours were enjoyable were the last 10, but the middle portion is too long and far less enjoyable. The main villain is far better than Calamity Ganon as are the dungeons. However, the gameplay loop is identical to Breath of the Wild.
much better than BOTW. but not much memorable about it. this will not be remembered as a masterpiece like ocarina.

I have this game from the beginning. I loved BotW, and I try and try to love this one, but i can’t. It’s too boring. The crafting kill everything for me. Moving objects with the controller is not intuitive. Even though I understand the puzzles, it takes me ages to apply the solution. And so i really have the feeling to lose my time. 
I really don’t understand how this game could have the same score as Botw. For me, it was such such better.

I don't know but maybe the hype created expectations that could not be met. I finished BoTW but could not finish this one. It just feels.. i don't know a bit thin. When I started this game after Witcher 3 I was really disapointed.

When I play a game I like the immersion. I don't want to feel like I am playing a game. But here they let you know it is a game and give you tools to exploit it. And there are like 3 types of enemies? 
If this was released on other consoles, they would not have a chance. The only reason for such a high score this gets is nostalgia and lack of competition. But could be fun for some. Just not for me .

Devastatingly uninspired retread of Breath of the Wild with a more formulaic Standard Zelda flavor. Fails as a sequel to Breath of the Wild, fails as a story, fails as a Zelda game, fails as an entry into the open world genre. It doesn't seem like the development team learned anything at all from Breath of the Wild except how to give Youtubers with too much time years' worth of budget Minecraft videos to make.

There's 43 goddamn underground abandoned mines in this game and they all look the same. And this game cost $70 United States Dollars.
Huge improvement over the prequel, but still not quite there to be worthy of being a new 3D Zelda adventure.

This game is overrated. It's mid at best. Yes, it has its fun moments but its questionable, and outright bad ones bring the game down to the point where the good moments are greatly overshadowed. Let's be honest. The depths ****. It's 80km^2 of the same copy-and-paste biome, fauna, and enemies all over with the occasional, sparsely placed structures like mines that don't really have anything interesting going on. Its design is the pinnacle of bad game design, resulting in a boring, tedious, and monotonous experience and gameplay loop. People saying that the depths are good are crazy, I just don't understand them at all. The same goes for the sky. It's mostly empty with copy-and-paste shrine puzzles. The biggest and most interesting part of the sky is ironically the tutorial island, but it's over quicker than one thinks. It sets the player's expectations on a misleading path, where one thinks they will see other sky islands like it later in the game, but behold, there are no other such places. The rest, again, are just isolated small, mostly copy-paste, islands with similar puzzles. It's so disappointing, considering the sky islands literally were the game's selling point. The ultra-hand mechanics are not thought out either. They allow for massive cheese in basically every puzzle and dungeon. It feels like I was cheesing more than actually doing the puzzles properly, and most puzzles outside of shrines in the game don't even require much interesting use of the ultra-hand mechanics anyway. It's overall just a non-thought-out gimmick. The shrines are terrible too. There are way too many tutorial and blessing shrines, way way too many. And the actual shrines are mostly boring and strange too, with way too much allowance for cheese, again. Back to the sky and depths, their lack of content does NOT help at all the fact that this game basically just copies BOTW's map. They added caves sure, but they're not that interesting considering the vast majority are the same; go in, break some rocks, find a frog, and get out. There are the occasional boss and shrine caves but they don't offer much variation either after you fight the same boss for the 10th time. They all look the same, play the same, and ultimately create again a boring, monotonous experience. What else have they added to the main map? Rocks! Copy and pasted everywhere. How interesting! And that's basically it aside from some minor changes here and there. It's all the same. The fact that the depths and the sky don't offer much intriguing discovery or exploration leads to this game heavily lacking in that aspect. It cannot stand on its own feet and leeches onto BOTW in order to seem "good" (it really isn't). It takes the same engine, map, assets, music, enemies, mechanics, and more and adds half-baked trash on top with just a tiny sprinkle of good. This game was in development for longer than its prequel yet it feels like they barely scratched the surface on making this a good overall stand-alone experience, especially considering that they already had the foundation with the engine, map, and mechanics. It feels more like a poorly made reimagining than an actual sequel, and the fact that you go to the same 4 exact places as in the first game doesn't help either. Don't get me started on the long copy-paste cutscene played 4 times. This game is weak, poorly made, and is overall a bad game by Nintendo. I expected way more from them, especially considering the development time, but here we are I guess And it's frustrating seeing how much praise this game gets when it does NOT deserve it at all, and Nintendo does NOT deserve their enormous earnings from it.

The things that make this Zelda and not **** were present in Breath Of The Wild. Just seems like cramming too many mechanics that are popular into a Zelda game, I can’t help but feel the same way about this as I do Banjo Kazooie nuts and bolts. Temples are present for OG fans but they are short and very easy to work out. The lack of challenge to this made it seem fairly redundant after a while and adding more of the same type of collectibles adds nothing to the overall gameplay. A good try but fell flat for me. Barely makes a Zelda top 10 list.
Again, it's got a great premise, and it could be so much more. I'd probably pay $5 for it as it stands, but not $15.

I was so hyped up for this, and finally got to play. But I've already played the same game before. The building-mechanic is cool, but some verticality and an underground were not delivering what I thought would be a unique experience building on top of BOTW's innovation. This should've been a large expansion or DLC, not a new game.

I think this game is extremely overrated as it’s literally breath of the wild copy and pasted. The new abilities were cool but I felt like they were kinda pointless. I didn’t really care for the building aspect of the game. The game just seemed very empty. There wasn’t anything in the sky and the depths were a complete waste of time. It’s fun if you want to explore but not worth the 70 bucks that it cost to buy. If it was a DLC I would’ve loved it. Overall, the game is very bland compared to other games and the story had nothing to do with the main storyline which was also disappointing. They need to go back to the original format with dungeons and heart pieces

A good story dlc , but yes its good cause its the same game as one of the best of all time .Yes the story is good , but bro like to repetitive 4 time 
the same things , with the same located dongeon as the one like why .
And the new technologies Soneau is like just mid compared to the sheika one .
A magnificent DLC but just a good games .

This game is great from the combat to the story. but almost everything in the game is borrowed from BOTW. this includes the map, the enemies, the combat system, the items, the mechanics, and the story. this game is honestly better than BOTW, but if you have already played it, this game ****. If you have not played BOTW, then play this game, but otherwise, this game is not worth the around 60 hours it takes to complete. also, why is it 70 dollars?!?!?!?!?!

Horrible garbage even before you compare it to breath of the wild. The ONLY things it does better than breath of the wild are music and cinematics. The story is bad, the gameplay, flow, and balancing are terrible, and there is an UNBELIEVABLE amount of copy paste in this game. Like, it's absolutely absurd.

Wow this game is really not it. Rather boring and makes absolutely zero sense in terms of story. The mechanics ****, the dungeons ****, and most interface components absolutely ****. There are so many unanswered questions. That said the music and visuals are amazing but also such was expected as they were amazing in the last game. Overall it feels like BOTW with some ok building mechanics I don't really care about and bad story elements that make no sense. BOTW feels tainted by the lacklustre release of TOTK

This is the only Zelda game I started but have no interest in finishing. The first hours are fun but wow does it get repetitive - especially the ultrahand building mechanic and frequent gathering/cooking. The new areas above/below ground don't add enough meaningful content so it just feels like you are replaying Breath of the Wild again.

Why is the map here so similar to the original map? It pretty much ruins the exploration. There were not enough sky islands at all... and the depths are just a reskin of the surface, kept very dark so you don't notice how boring and empty it is. the story is genuinely horrible, I've watched SpongeBob episodes with a better plot than this game. and the dungeons in this game are pretty bad, though not as bad as in Breath of the Wild. This game also completely lacks an atmosphere of any kind. it evokes little to no emotion through a majority of the game

The good stuff? I like the enemy variety and combat, and I like the fuse mechanic a lot. Some of the puzzles are pretty decent, and the sand temple almost felt like an actual dungeon with fun stuff in it. almost. 

Ganondorf was a good villain, and his boss sequence is one of the best in the entire Zelda series. That said, this game was a massive, massive disappointment. Nintendo needs to set their next Zelda game in a very different world, with new exploration to be done. And hopefully, it might have a decent story and atmosphere to go along with it. 

I recommend playing this game if you've never played Breathe of the Wild, as then the exploration will feel fresh. Otherwise, play at your own risk

personally this just feels like a copy and paste that tries to expand but fails, the sky areas feel the same and bland, nothing about the ground changes. But the story is much better but the cons outweigh the pros sadly.

For the most part it's kind of entertaining but that's it, it's just entertaining. There's so much to do but the stuff that you're actually doing feels kinda superficial. I notice my brain switches off while playing, the same way it does while watching stuff like tiktok or other short form media because all the things you do in this game seem like short bursts of random things to that don't really amount to anything, you don't feel like you're progressing towards an overarching goal like you do in other Zelda games, you're just fulfilling what the game has to offer for the sake of it, for that little bit of dopamine you get after completing a shrine or what ever copy paste things you can find in the world. And the parts of the game that are supposed to progress the story, the dungeons, just feel lazily tacked on. They don't really feel part of the world they're in other than the fire temple and the lightning temple. You don't get that feeling slowly unraveling a dungeon that feels rewarding when you beat it, instead all you have to do it walk up to the terminal things which are marked on your map and that's it. It feels kinda naff. And on top of that the cutscenes after are so lame. I've never seen something so cheesy. It just kinda feels like it doesn't take itself seriously, which is upsetting because that's not what I liked Zelda for when I was younger. It doesn't even feel like a Zelda game, just an open world type thing vaguely trying to disguise itself as a zelda game.

Everything *Breath of the Wild* did fantastically is still present here but not expanded upon. Five years for a glorified DLC and a $70 price tag is insane. They didn't fix any of the issues with *Breath of the Wild*, mainly the dungeons. While the dungeons have been improved upon, they are still weak in the grand scheme of Zelda dungeons.

The world being recycled is fine, I guess. The underworld is cool until you realize they copied the map, inverted the Y-axis, painted it black, spammed annoying enemies around, and added roots. There are good aspects to the depths, like the little built areas that are cool, and the cosmetics have been replaced from paid microtransactions (Amiibos) to in-game drops. The sky is boring and repetitive. Really, the only redeeming factor of this game is the building, which is fantastic, don't get me wrong, but it's just fun factor. The story, dungeons, environments, and bosses are barely different from *Breath of the Wild*. It's an alright game, but *Breath of the Wild* was groundbreaking. A $70 DLC is kind of ridiculous. If you enjoyed *Breath of the Wild*, you will like this, but personally, I'm already tired of the new formula and a game that was originally meant to be a DLC isn't really for me. I enjoyed this game and don't regret playing it or buying it, but I'm a little disappointed with some of it. 

7/10. It's a good game, that's it.

Sequel to BOTW yet nothing truly innovative was added. Nice, you can build stuff. That's cool and fuse mechanic is amazing.

However, the gameloop is exactly the same, and exploration is very unrewarding. Collect koroks, shrines, and dungeons exactly like the last game. it honestly feels like a mod rather than a true sequel. 

Makes the game feel bloated rather than a true zelda game filled with wonder and exploration. Feels like a chore to play. Underground is a repetitive disappointment, same with the over world (wow! another korok that says the exact same thing! with the same reward! wow!!!!) , same with the falsely advertised sky islands (what a joke). 


Every task just becomes another MMO problem of expecting a number to go up. No unexpected rewards, no story immersive context or quests. Genuinely blows my mind they made the game like this. 

6 years for this? And let's talk about the story. A sequel that tries very hard to forget the old one, probably to get new fans that don't require any context to play a sequel. Cowards who played safe and forgot what made BOTW so special (hint : it wasnt the repetitive meaningless korok and shrine collection). 
****  you
but the game is beautiful btw. lol.

I was very much looking forward to this game. But the switch in focus from the first to the sequel turned me off. I get that combat needed to be harder but I loved the more exploration focused nature of the first game. It seemed like this sequel was all about the combat system and nothing else. Sure smashing stuff together to build things is fun at first, but when you get one shot by pretty much every enemy you find it gets pretty old fast. Also stop breaking the weapons, or at least give some way to repair them. I hate feeling like I can't use a weapon because I don't want it to break yet. The original was in all way better.

Hiya folks, welcome to this week's episode of "way too late reviews", where I'll be reviewing The Legend of Zelda : Tears of the Kingdom, the sequel to the legally distinct 2017 game, Breath of the Wild. There are rocks in the sky now! Seriously though, it does feel different. There are improvements, for sure. New monster types are cool, bokoblins wearing armour is cool, fusing stuff is cool, ultrahand is cool, coolcoolcool. I have some minor gripes about these things, but for the most part, the game builds on BotW with success. But there's a lot of stuff that annoys me. The intro ****. BotW's Great Plateau was a great tutorial section, it gave players a shocking amount of freedom while teaching the basics, and I just don't think the Great Sky Island works nearly as well. The sky islands don't allow that kind of freedom, and it feels restrictive. At some point I was kinda stuck, and figured I should just warp back to a different point to take a different path, but the warp ability is locked until later. Why? It's harder to navigate the islands than the plateau, and BotW has the teleport ability available eariler. Finally, I leave the islands, get back to the ground below, and I got no map. Clearly there's going to be towers, but I don't see them. Sure, Purah will tell me, but I have to spend another 10 minutes walking around talking to people before I get to check it out. Now Purah gives me the paraglider so I can be shot up into the sky from a tower to get the local map info, which is cool. But at the same time, the game doesn't really incentivize the type of exploration that made the first game so fun. The shrines are harder to see, instead of glowing a bright orange they're surrounded by this greenish mist that doesn't pop at all. I used to spend a few minutes at the top of a tower scoping the area for bright spots. Now, I jump into the air and try to pick a destination within a few seconds. Once I've hit one of these targets, I would usually end up teleporting back to the tower to start the cycle over again. I don't have a sensor, I can't see any shrines, I don't have any armour worth mentioning, I'm low on weapons, just going off isn't viable. I never felt like walking aimlessly like I did in BotW. Walking aimlessly sounds bad, but BotW actually made it good. Now, the loop of catapulting high into the sky and paragliding over to a shrine doesn't promote that relaxing, interesting, secret-discovering walking that was central to BotW. And I get it, it can't be the same as in BotW, but this new paradigm of travel rarely produces that feeling of wonder that BotW seemed to be able to dole out with such consistency: seeing a Divine Beast for the first time; catching my first glimpse of a dragon; climbing the Akkala citadel; seeing a goddamn bear. That's a hard act to follow, and I don't want to be disingenuous. But I gotta callz'em like I seez'em, and I kept seeing a game that prevented me from doing what I wanted to do. Where's my shrine sensor? I got to this tower, and it didn't work. Gotta make some porridge before buying stealth armour. A Hyrule-sized map of the Typhlo ruins (the Depths) isn't my idea of fun. Caves almost all feel and look the same. The memories don't force me discover the world like they did in BotW. Spears are OP now with fusion, and 2-handed weapons kinda ****. I don't care about Bubbulfrogs. The sky islands look cool until you've seen the same one five times. Shrine rewards are garbage, I don't want a large battery. Speaking of armour, I'm 50 hours in, where are my upgrades? I had to check a guide to activate a fairy fountain, and I needed to do 4 (FOUR!) quests to see 1 (ONE!) fairy. AND THEN I GET CHARGED TO UPGRADE!? Preposterous! Scandalous! Ignominious! In a game where random exploration should be enjoyable, why would I want to get bogged down with these restrictive quests? Why do I have to do THIS specific Main Quest event to get access to fairies? Why am I being punished for doing them out of order? BotW didn't have this, this, this video game equivalent of c*ckblocking. Why are there so many item types? Why do I need 75 pieces of armour if I only use like 5 different sets? Why can't I just put on a whole set at a time yet? Why does enemy damage seem way out of whack? Why do I need colour-specific enemy drops for upgrade when I have 75 armour parts to upgrade? I haven't seen a red Lynel in about 40 hours, how am I supposed to find its specific parts? Why are enemy horns in the codex but not fangs? Why can't I fuse items to my weapons or shields in the menu? Why can't I scroll through the menu with the right stick anymore? Why do the avatars ****? WHY WHY WHY WHYYYYY I just don't really want to return to it. I'm done with it. It's obviously not a bad game, but ultimately I gotta tell Mari Kondo that BotW sparks joy, and TotK does not.

TOTK is just a much worse version of BOTW but with cooler mechanics.

Story is terrible, maybe even the worst in any game I've ever played. In BOTW, the story, environments, and various vibes were what kept the gameplay repetitiveness in check. In TOTK, the story feels like nothing but repetitiveness (and insult to the player's intelligence).

Even the repetitive gameplay itself is more repetitive in TOTK, with much less effort going into creating a unique experience for each location -- despite already having the locations set up. What variety there is feels novel for about 1 or 2 minutes before it becomes clear it is going to be the same gimmick for the rest of the experience.

I had high expectations for this sequel to Zelda: Breath of the Wild. I am satisfied with the various functions, but I can't find the liveliness of the previous game, the game difficulty is at a child's level, and the feeling of the game being forced together made me quit the game after clearing the four gods.
Is the worst Zelda of all time. The story doesn't fit into the Zelda universe at all, because the weapons stop breaking and there are no more items or music.

Well made, fun for a while, but it feels empty because they don't care about the lore, don't care about linking it even to breath of the wild, so what was mysterious and filled with accumulated history in botw is set dressing here.

After spending 1000h on BOTW, i couldn't spend more than 80h on TOTK. Even if its 5 years of work, it still feels too much DLC of BOTW... I did 2 dungeons and from one day to another, just lost interrest. I can't put my head on it again. It's the very first Zelda that i will probably never finish. I simply don't really like the direction this serie is taking, and i hope the next game will reimplement it's traditional recipe, and get rid of this weapon durability, and korok seeds. Ninjas are using weapons like disposable items, they have no value to them. Samuraïs have a deep respect and care for their katana, they take good care of it. Well, i'm more like a Samuraï on this sense... and i know that we are 50% Ninjas 50% Samuraïs on this question between the fans.
ah yes, yet another open-world game... kinda tired of them. Endlessly running from destination to destination gets old.

It's fun for a couple of hours, until you realize how shallow and repetitive it is. Nintendo has lost their way and it baffles me how well the Switch is doing, considering how many **** games they've put out the last few years.

For Tears of the Kingdom, my experience playing through the game has been bad.  I have nothing against TotK using the same map as BotW but with 2 added landscape to explore, the sky and depths.  Exploring the world of TotK seems rewarding at first, but it starts getting old too fast and the only reward is just seeing the sites.  You still have to find hundreds of Korok seeds to expand your weapon, bows, and shields inventories to functional levels.  There are physical dungeons, but they don't hold the charm as the traditional Zelda games achieved.

The most important factors of any game is the content and how you play in it.  The content is more quantity than quality.  The gameplay feels good if you have plenty of resources to burn through.  Finding great weapons is a bit more difficult in TotK than BotW due to new mechanics from the Zonai technology.  To incentivize using the fusion mechanic, weapons have lower durability than they had in BotW.  As long as you have decent fusion material, you can make "decent" weapons, but for me, that fusion material pool is used for too much, and this led to terrible experiences with combat.  If I want a better time dealing with tough encounters, it involved spending hours in the depths farming for bombs and materials.

Overall, the game play is more punishing than Dark Souls type games.  If this is the future of Zelda games, the series is doomed.

Bloated. Seriously underwhelming. Though, Much better than BOTW, which was ultimately empty and story-less and the beginning of this sandbox era of Zelda games... This doesn't suit the serious (imo) ... Breath of the Wild was overrated for what was offered, I don't recommend these two games as representative of what a true Zelda game is. Majora's Mask, Twilight Princess, Ocarina of Time even Skyward Sword does Zelda justice not this. There's open world and then there's overcooked... Been a fan since Ocarina its a shame to see my interest in Zelda drop almost completely. I like Rauru and the whole cast its just overshadowed by gameplay that is laughable, creative but too cartoonish and overall without a sense of honour and direction

Not enough built off of BOTW. Poor story, easy mechanics, rewards pointless, failed to live up to its hype. If you ranked BOTW a ten it would be easy to do so with Tears because it's the same game, but everything new with some minor exceptions falls far below par. Especially the lore and history they built with BOTW.
This game was a lil too easy I did all sidequest and side adventures and main quest in about 2 days. I was hoping for more of a challenge. Breath of the wild was harder than this zelda to be honest.

Pure quantity over quality. The game is like, 200 hours long and I discovered everything in the first 5 - the rest was all copy and paste.

I will never understand why people like this game.

While the game functions fine technically, I don't have much else positive to say. TotK is everything BotW was not, it's unrewarding, grindy, immersion-breaking, poorly written, trend-chasing, un-interactive, and, frankly, stupid. 

The core gameplay loop of exploration doesn't work without a world designed for that exploration, that the player has not experienced and does not fully understand. That was BotW innovation. TotK fails to understand this on a fundamental level to such a degree that we might even start to think BotW was a fluke. 

Beyond that, TotK's new mechanics actually negatively impact BotW's other major innovation: world interaction. Because, you no longer have to think about the terrain when you can just Ascend past it all or build a flying machine to bypass it all. It's kind of amazing how little they considered BotW when making this game.

Then, there's the story, which tries to pretend all other Zelda games never existed. BotW included, despite being a sequel. Never seen anything like it before, but it's awful.

I wanted to enjoy ToTK. But, unfortunately, this is the first Zelda game I've ever disliked playing. There are too many things that didn't click with me. The weapon durability, the koroks, the reused map, and having to practically reunlock every tower and shrine. I actually hated the building function. I hate having to build things in games. I'm mostly just disappointed. With the game, but mostly with myself for not being able to enjoy it.
This Game is just Zelda Breath of The Wild but In worse. Not worth the Price. I dont like this Game

Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom is a colossal misstep that strays so far from the beloved formula of the Zelda series that it feels like a different, inferior game altogether. The uninspired narrative, lackluster visuals, poorly implemented open-world mechanics, and technical shortcomings collectively make Tears of the Kingdom the worst entry in the Zelda franchise, tarnishing the legacy of a series that has long been celebrated for its innovation, charm, and timeless appeal.

Just a Bad Game. They put no effort into this DLC. This is no real Zelda Game. No real Dungeons. Just a bunch of repetitive Micro Dungeons wich all look the same. There is just no soul in this Game anymore. An almost Empty World. And no Items like they used to be. But now we can glue a Rock to a Stick. Wow. I can only hope they make a real Game sometime in the Future again.

A **** game that does not deserve any recognition at all.Although it is billed as a sequel to the previous Breath of the wild, there is no sense of connection between the story lines, and the scenario is so bad that it ignores the settings and stories of each of the previous games in the series. Even when viewed as a stand-alone game, it is a garbage scenario full of contradictions and leaves no **** controls and UI are also unpleasant, and the software does not have the minimum quality that should be fulfilled.There is nothing good about the design, which is not easy to see, or the BGM, which leaves no impression at all, even when viewed as an art form.This is one of the rarest and most boring games in recent years.

(I am not good at English so I use translation software. There may be some oddities.)

Dropped this game for the desperation right after the tutorial. This is a DLC of Bow, nothing more. Having already played Bow, and i was hoping to get at least a game that tried to make up for its flaws, instead all the boredom is there again. 80% of gameplay time is wasted in trying to stick things and navigating in bad designed menus, the remaining 20% is walking around the same environment of the last game, grinding for resources and navigating the map. This is not fun, but merely content to be consumed by fanboys
Dreadfully boring. Yes you can interact with the environment in many unique ways and while unique is something games should strive for.. Uniqueness does not on its own equal fun.
Just more of the same from BOTW. I do not recommend this at all

Only thing that this game has going for it is the fact it can run on a toaster machine,

I guess the UI is nice too.

Too long, too boring, it has too much hype but don't reach the expectations. 
I've played it long enough to finish the campaign but yet it was lacking of a deep story and new mechanics, although it has a minecraft-like building system, it is not that good, you can be creative but that is all, there is an exciting combat system or a new systems that would get you hooked to the game as much as possible.

People are going too soft on this game since it got review-bombed with 10s at the start and they're probably afraid of offending people. It's not just - "Im too old for this!" (Thats an excuse to calm the internet). As a 26-year-old, I can still enjoy many other games with more intensive puzzle mechanics/building. A game where I have to watch a youtube video to make every contraption I need to make is a bad game. I get absolutely no reward for tediously gluing parts together for hours so to complete this slog I just ended up climbing and using stamina potions just like the last game. Call me an idiot, but I don't have time to keep failing, and failing just to save another Korok and get two pieces of poop. I've been a Zelda fan for decades and played and enjoyed every title thus far (even BOTW! Though I enjoyed it less than many others). TOTK is "fun" in the sense that sometimes I feel like I get to do something interesting, but lets face it - for the most part this game is a puzzle/engineering sim THEMED with Zelda. And if you dont like spending hours upon hours doing literal work for minor rewards, this game isn't for you. We watched people playing it online - tons of videos with robots shooting lasers from their nether regions and thought haha! That looks fun. But the strangest thing happened. My partner and I get the game, played for an afternoon, and then moved on with our lives. Then I went to plug in my Wii and play Twilight Princess again ;) When the hype clears and your depression over the world's events has its upturn - which game do you return to? Probably not TOTK.
Also where's this metacritic score coming from? 96? Really? That is hilarious. Nice job lining your pockets with Nintendo money.
Horrible glitchy sequel that adds nothing to botw and is overall a snooze fest and tedious to get through horrible video game definitely not goty
the console cant handle the game. the console launched at 2017 and since then had no upgrades. this games runs at 30 fps with below standard graphics in 2023

This game was a nightmare to experience.  The English voice actors were so irritating I had to turn the voice acting into a different language.  The camera controls were awful no matter how I set them.  And the tutorial level is slow, and full of obtuse puzzle obstacles which don't make you feel clever for solving them, just frustrated.  There was one place in the tutorial level where you can get stuck if you happen to fall off the icy ledge before getting the Ascend ability, WHICH could have been easily remedied if they enabled warping in the tutorial level.  Two hours of non-stop frustration.

Same game. A few new mechanics which are so incredibly "meh". Can't stand this washed-out "art style". Nintendo, give your devs some actual hardware, and maybe everything won't need to be stale and cell-shaded.

Can't believe they didn't just make this thing a DLC.

Well basically repeats the same scenario from the previous game. Of course the grafics looks annoying for the Nintendo switch device. The story is good, very very long game too had minimum 200 hours of your time.

Wow! I can’t believe that I would ever give a Zelda game such a low rating. But honestly after playing Zelda for 30 yrs I can honestly say I miss the old style of Zelda gameplay. I like the open world but, I HATE WEAPON DURABILITY. I also don’t really care for the new fusing of things. The new game isn’t for me and I won’t be buying the next game if it’s more of the same. I also miss old dungeons and how you had to progress through the story and open a chest and find your hook shot or mirror shield.
SORRY NINTENDO I DONT LIKE THE NEW GAME!

I'm genuinely disappointed with this new entry. I had high hopes for it and was brimming with excitement, especially after falling in love with BOTW. However, what I encountered was a mere replica with an excessive emphasis on farming and grinding, making it less enjoyable. While I do commend the level of freedom granted for weapon crafting and vehicles, the mechanics underlying these aspects feel remarkably cumbersome. It always seems like you're awkwardly piecing things together, and the game doesn't become engaging until you finally acquire a few batteries. Yet, the process of obtaining them is far from enjoyable, requiring three Zonaite just to obtain a single Crystallized Charge, and an astonishing 300 for a mere one-third battery upgrade. The substantial time investment in this farming aspect is simply not fun.

Furthermore, performance issues plague the entire game, which is surprising given that many reviewers appear to overlook these problems. It's evident that these issues, if present in any other game, would have garnered severe criticism. When i looked for advice online i found some players telling me to "Set TV output in the System Menu to 480p. and only play on docked mode"... I am no graphic **** by any means I swear to you but I suppose I can expect more than that in 2023, especially after they raised their prices specifically for TOTK knowing we will buy it anyway. Adding to my disappointment, the game's structure mirrors that of BOTW precisely. The novelty that once existed seems to have dissipated, likely contributing to my disenchantment. The puzzle rooms also got stale pretty quickly this time around. While I'm still intrigued by the future of the Zelda series, I earnestly hope for an innovation that delivers a completely fresh experience this time around. However, considering the overwhelming success of TOTK, I'm wary of setting my expectations too high. I realized that my score might seem harsh but ultimately the game was disappointing to me to the point where I almost dropped it before the very end so here we are!
